files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.CancelEvent
files.Summary: is emitted when the user initiates a cancellation of the update in progress, or the update successfully completes.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.DiagnosticEvent
files.Summary: is emitted whenever a diagnostic message is provided, for example errors from a cloud resource provider while trying to create or update a resource.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.DiffKind
files.Summary: describes the kind of a particular property diff.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.EngineEvent
files.Summary: describes a Pulumi engine event, such as a change to a resource or diagnostic message. EngineEvent is a discriminated union of all possible event types, and exactly one field will be non-null.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.PolicyEvent
files.Summary: is emitted whenever there is Policy violation.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.PreludeEvent
files.Summary: is emitted at the start of an update.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.PropertyDiff
files.Summary: describes the difference between a single property's old and new values.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.ResourceOperationFailedEvent
files.Summary: is emitted when a resource operation fails. Typically a is emitted before this event, indiciating what the root cause of the error.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.ResourceOutputsEvent
files.Summary: is emitted when a resource is finished being provisioned.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.ResourcePreEvent
files.Summary: is emitted before a resource is modified.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.StandardOutputEngineEvent
files.Summary: is emitted whenever a generic message is written, for example warnings from the pulumi CLI itself. Less common than .

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.StepEventMetadata
files.Summary: describes a "step" within the Pulumi engine, which is any concrete action to migrate a set of cloud resources from one state to another.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.StepEventStateMetadata
files.Summary: is the more detailed state information for a resource as it relates to a step(s) being performed.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Events.SummaryEvent
files.Summary: is emitted at the end of an update, with a summary of the changes made.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Exceptions.ProjectSettingsConflictException
files.Summary:
Thrown when creating a Workspace detects a conflict between project settings found on disk (such as Pulumi.yaml) and a ProjectSettings object passed to the Create API.

There are two resolutions:

(A) to use the ProjectSettings, delete the Pulumi.yaml file from WorkDir or use a different WorkDir

(B) to use the exiting Pulumi.yaml from WorkDir, avoid customizing the ProjectSettings

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ExecutorImage
files.Summary: Information about the remote execution image.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.HistoryOptions
files.Summary: Options controlling the behavior of a operation.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.LocalProgramArgs
files.Summary: Description of a stack backed by pre-existing local Pulumi CLI program.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.LocalWorkspace
files.Summary:
LocalWorkspace is a default implementation of the Workspace interface.

A Workspace is the execution context containing a single Pulumi project, a program, and multiple stacks.Workspaces are used to manage the execution environment, providing various utilities such as plugin installation, environment configuration ($PULUMI_HOME), and creation, deletion, and listing of Stacks.

LocalWorkspace relies on Pulumi.yaml and Pulumi.{stack}.yaml as the intermediate format for Project and Stack settings.Modifying ProjectSettings will alter the Workspace Pulumi.yaml file, and setting config on a Stack will modify the Pulumi.{stack}.yaml file. This is identical to the behavior of Pulumi CLI driven workspaces.

If not provided a working directory - causing LocalWorkspace to create a temp directory, than the temp directory will be cleaned up on .

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.LocalWorkspaceOptions
files.Summary: Extensibility options to configure a LocalWorkspace; e.g: settings to seed and environment variables to pass through to every command.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.PendingCreateValue
files.Summary: Config values for Importing Pending Create operations

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.PreviewOptions

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ProjectBackend

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ProjectRuntime

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ProjectRuntimeName

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ProjectRuntimeOptions
files.Summary: Various configuration options that apply to different language runtimes.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ProjectSettings

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ProjectTemplate

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.ProjectTemplateConfigValue

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.RemoteGitAuthArgs
files.Summary:
Authentication options for the repository that can be specified for a private Git repo. There are three different authentication paths:

  • Personal accesstoken
  • SSH private key (and its optional password)
  • Basic auth username and password

Only one authentication path is valid.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.RemoteGitProgramArgs
files.Summary: Description of a stack backed by a remote Pulumi program in a Git repository.

files.Summary: Common options controlling the behavior of update actions taken against an instance of .

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.RemoteWorkspaceOptions
files.Summary: Extensibility options to configure a RemoteWorkspace.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.SecretsProviderOptions
files.Summary: Options to pass into .

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.StackDeployment
files.Summary:
Represents the state of a stack deployment as used by ExportStackAsync and ImportStackAsync.

There is no strongly typed model for the contents yet, but you can access the raw representation via the Json property.

NOTE: instances may contain sensitive data (secrets).

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.UpOptions
files.Summary: Options controlling the behavior of an operation.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.UpdateOptions
files.Summary: Common options controlling the behavior of update actions taken against an instance of .

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.Workspace
files.Summary:
Workspace is the execution context containing a single Pulumi project, a program, and multiple stacks.

Workspaces are used to manage the execution environment, providing various utilities such as plugin installation, environment configuration ($PULUMI_HOME), and creation, deletion, and listing of Stacks.

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.WorkspaceStack
files.Summary:
is an isolated, independently configurable instance of a Pulumi program. exposes methods for the full pulumi lifecycle (up/preview/refresh/destroy), as well as managing configuration.

Multiple stacks are commonly used to denote different phases of development (such as development, staging, and production) or feature branches (such as feature-x-dev, jane-feature-x-dev).

Will dispose the on .

files.Title: Pulumi.Automation.WorkspaceStackState
files.Summary: Module class for manipulating stack state for a given .

files.Title: Pulumi.Alias
files.Summary:
Alias is a description of prior named used for a resource. It can be processed in the context of a resource creation to determine what the full aliased URN would be.

Use in the case where a prior URN is known and can just be specified in full. Otherwise, provide some subset of the other properties in this type to generate an appropriate urn from the pre-existing values of the with certain parts overridden.

The presence of a property indicates if its value should be used. If absent (i.e. null), then the value is not used.

Note: because of the above, there needs to be special handling to indicate that the previous of a was null. Specifically, pass in:

Aliases = { new Alias { NoParent = true } }

files.Title: Pulumi.Archive
files.Summary: An Archive represents a collection of named assets.

files.Title: Pulumi.Asset
files.Summary: Asset represents a single blob of text or data that is managed as a first class entity.

files.Title: Pulumi.AssetArchive
files.Summary: An AssetArchive is an archive created from an in-memory collection of named assets or other archives.

files.Title: Pulumi.AssetOrArchive
files.Summary: Base class of s and s.

files.Title: Pulumi.CallArgs
files.Summary: Base type for all call argument classes.

files.Title: Pulumi.CallOptions
files.Summary: Options to help control the behavior of .

files.Title: Pulumi.ComponentResource
files.Summary: A that aggregates one or more other child resources into a higher level abstraction. The component resource itself is a resource, but does not require custom CRUD operations for provisioning.

files.Title: Pulumi.ComponentResourceOptions
files.Summary: A bag of optional settings that control a 's behavior.

files.Title: Pulumi.Config
files.Summary: is a bag of related configuration state. Each bag contains any number of configuration variables, indexed by simple keys, and each has a name that uniquely identifies it; two bags with different names do not share values for variables that otherwise share the same key. For example, a bag whose name is pulumi:foo, with keys a, b, and c, is entirely separate from a bag whose name is pulumi:bar with the same simple key names. Each key has a fully qualified names, such as pulumi:foo:a, ..., and pulumi:bar:a, respectively.

files.Title: Pulumi.CustomResource
files.Summary: CustomResource is a resource whose create, read, update, and delete (CRUD) operations are managed by performing external operations on some physical entity. The engine understands how to diff and perform partial updates of them, and these CRUD operations are implemented in a dynamically loaded plugin for the defining package.

files.Title: Pulumi.CustomResourceOptions
files.Summary: is a bag of optional settings that control a 's behavior.

files.Title: Pulumi.CustomTimeouts
files.Summary: Optional timeouts to supply in .

files.Title: Pulumi.DeferredOutput<T>
files.Summary: Represents the producer side of an value, providing access to the consumer side through the Output property.

files.Title: Pulumi.Deployment
files.Summary:
is the entry-point to a Pulumi application. .NET applications should perform all startup logic they need in their Main method and then end with:

static Task<int> Main(string[] args) { // program initialization code ... 

return Deployment.Run(async () =>
{
    // Code that creates resources.
});

}

Importantly: Cloud resources cannot be created outside of the lambda passed to any of the overloads. Because cloud Resource construction is inherently asynchronous, the result of this function is a which should then be returned or awaited. This will ensure that any problems that are encountered during the running of the program are properly reported. Failure to do this may lead to the program ending early before all resources are properly registered.

files.Title: Pulumi.DeploymentInstance
files.Summary: Metadata of the deployment that is currently running. Accessible via .

files.Title: Pulumi.DictionaryInvokeArgs
files.Summary:
A special type of with resource inputs represented as a loosely-typed dictionary of objects. Normally, should not be used by resource providers since it's too low-level and provides low safety. Its target scenario are resources with a very dynamic shape of inputs. The input dictionary may only contain objects that are serializable by Pulumi, i.e only the following types (or pulumi.Output of those types) are allowed:

  • Primitive types: , , ,
  • , , or
  • Generic collections of the above: , with keys,

files.Title: Pulumi.DictionaryResourceArgs
files.Summary:
A special type of with resource inputs represented as a loosely-typed dictionary of objects. Normally, should not be used by resource providers since it's too low-level and provides low safety. Its target scenario are resources with a very dynamic shape of inputs. The input dictionary may only contain objects that are serializable by Pulumi, i.e only the following types (or pulumi.Output of those types) are allowed:

  • Primitive types: , , ,
  • , , or
  • Generic collections of the above: , with keys,

files.Title: Pulumi.EnumTypeAttribute
files.Summary:
Attribute used by a Pulumi Cloud Provider Package to mark enum types.

Requirements for a struct-based enum to be (de)serialized are as follows. It must:

  • Be a value type (struct) decoratted with EnumTypeAttribute.
  • Have a constructor that takes a single parameter of the underlying type. The constructor can be private.
  • Have an explicit conversion operator that converts the enum type to the underlying type.
  • Have an underlying type of String or Double.
  • Implementing IEquatable, adding ==/=! operators and overriding ToString isn't required, but is recommended and is what our codegen does.

files.Title: Pulumi.ErrorHook
files.Summary: An error hook is a callback that can be registered to run when a resource operation fails. The callback can return true to retry the operation or false to not retry.

files.Title: Pulumi.ErrorHookArgs
files.Summary:
ErrorHookArgs represents the arguments passed to an error hook. Depending on the failed operation, only some of the new/old inputs/outputs are set. 

| Failed Operation | old_inputs | new_inputs | old_outputs |
| ---------------- | ---------- | ---------- | ----------- |
| create           |            | ✓          |             |
| update           | ✓          | ✓          | ✓           |
| delete           | ✓          |            | ✓           |

files.Title: Pulumi.ErrorHookCallback
files.Summary: ErrorHookCallback is a delegate that defines the signature of callback functions. Returns true to retry the operation, false to not retry.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.IEngine
files.Summary: An interface to the engine host running this plugin.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.LogRequest
files.Summary: A log message to be sent to the Pulumi engine.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.LogSeverity
files.Summary: LogSeverity is the severity level of a log message. Errors are fatal; all others are informational.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.PropertyValueType
files.Summary: Property values will be one of these types.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.Provider.ComponentAnalyzer
files.Summary: Analyzes component resource types and generates a package schema.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.Provider.ComponentProvider
files.Summary: A provider that can be used to construct components from a given assembly with automatic schema inference.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.Provider.ComponentProviderHost
files.Summary: A host to serve the component provider automatically. See for more details.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.Provider.ParametersArgs
files.Summary: A parameter value, represented as an array of strings, as might be provided by a command-line invocation, such as that used to generate an SDK.

files.Title: Pulumi.Experimental.Provider.ParametersValue
files.Summary: A parameter value, represented by an arbitrary array of bytes accompanied by a name and version. This is expected to be the format used by parameterized provider SDKs.

files.Title: Pulumi.FileArchive
files.Summary: A FileArchive is a file-based archive, or a collection of file-based assets. This can be a raw directory or a single archive file in one of the supported formats(.tar, .tar.gz, or.zip).

files.Title: Pulumi.FileAsset
files.Summary: FileAsset is a kind of asset produced from a given path to a file on the local filesystem.

files.Title: Pulumi.Input<T>
files.Summary: is a property input for a . It may be a promptly available T, or the output from a existing .

files.Title: Pulumi.InputArgs
files.Summary: Base type for all input argument classes.

files.Title: Pulumi.InputAttribute
files.Summary:
Attribute used by a Pulumi Cloud Provider Package to mark input fields and properties.

Note: for simple inputs (i.e. this should just be placed on the property itself. i.e. [Input] Input<string> Acl.

For collection inputs (i.e. this should be placed on the backing field for the property. i.e.

[Input] private InputList<string> _acls;
public InputList<string> Acls
{
    get => _acls ?? (_acls = new InputList<string>());
    set => _acls = value;
}

files.Title: Pulumi.InputJson
files.Summary: Represents an value that wraps a .

files.Title: Pulumi.InputList<T>
files.Summary:
A list of values that can be passed in as the arguments to a . The individual values are themselves s. i.e. the individual values can be concrete values or s.

differs from a normal in that it is itself an . For example, a that accepts an will accept not just a list but an of a list. This is important for cases where the list from some needs to be passed into another . Or for cases where creating the list invariably produces an because its resultant value is dependent on other s.

This benefit of is also a limitation. Because it represents a list of values that may eventually be created, there is no way to simply iterate over, or access the elements of the list synchronously.

is designed to be easily used in object and collection initializers. For example, a resource that accepts a list of inputs can be written in either of these forms: 

new SomeResource("name", new SomeResourceArgs {
    ListProperty = { Value1, Value2, Value3 },
});

or

new SomeResource("name", new SomeResourceArgs {
    ListProperty = new [] { Value1, Value2, Value3 },
});

files.Title: Pulumi.InputMap<TValue>
files.Summary:
A mapping of s to values that can be passed in as the arguments to a . The individual values are themselves s. i.e. the individual values can be concrete values or s.

differs from a normal in that it is itself an . For example, a that accepts an will accept not just a dictionary but an of a dictionary as well. This is important for cases where the map from some needs to be passed into another . Or for cases where creating the map invariably produces an because its resultant value is dependent on other s.

This benefit of is also a limitation. Because it represents a list of values that may eventually be created, there is no way to simply iterate over, or access the elements of the map synchronously.

is designed to be easily used in object and collection initializers. For example, a resource that accepts a map of values can be written easily in this form: 

new SomeResource("name", new SomeResourceArgs {
    MapProperty = {
        { Key1, Value1 },
        { Key2, Value2 },
        { Key3, Value3 },
    },
});

files.Title: Pulumi.InputUnion<T0, T1>
files.Summary: Represents an value that can be one of two different types. For example, it might potentially be an some of the time or a in other cases.

files.Title: Pulumi.InvokeArgs
files.Summary: Base type for all invoke argument classes.

files.Title: Pulumi.InvokeOptions
files.Summary: Options to help control the behavior of .

files.Title: Pulumi.InvokeOutputOptions
files.Summary: Options to help control the behavior of .

files.Title: Pulumi.InvokeTransform
files.Summary: The type of an invoke transform.

files.Title: Pulumi.Log
files.Summary: Logging functions that can be called from a .NET application that will be logged to the Pulumi log stream. These events will be printed in the terminal while the Pulumi app runs, and will be available from the Web console afterwards.

files.Title: Pulumi.LogException
files.Summary: Special exception we throw if we had a problem actually logging a message to the engine error rpc endpoint. In this case, we have no choice but to tear ourselves down reporting whatever we can to the console instead.

files.Title: Pulumi.Output<T>
files.Summary:
s are a key part of how Pulumi tracks dependencies between s. Because the values of outputs are not available until resources are created, these are represented using the special s type, which internally represents two things: an eventually available value of the output and the dependency on the source(s) of the output value. In fact, s is quite similar to . Additionally, they carry along dependency information.

The output properties of all resource objects in Pulumi have type .

files.Title: Pulumi.Output
files.Summary: Useful static utility methods for both creating and working with s.

files.Title: Pulumi.OutputAttribute
files.Summary: Attribute used by a mark output properties. Use this attribute in your Pulumi programs to mark outputs of and resources.

files.Title: Pulumi.OutputConstructorAttribute
files.Summary:
Attribute used by a Pulumi Cloud Provider Package to marks the constructor for a complex property type so that it can be instantiated by the Pulumi runtime.

The constructor should contain parameters that map to the resultant returned by the engine.

files.Title: Pulumi.OutputConstructorParameterAttribute
files.Summary: Attribute used by a Pulumi Cloud Provider Package to mark constructor parameters with a name override.

files.Title: Pulumi.OutputExtensions
files.Summary: Extension methods for .

files.Title: Pulumi.OutputTypeAttribute
files.Summary: Attribute used by a Pulumi Cloud Provider Package to mark complex types used for a Resource output property. A complex type must have a single constructor in it marked with the attribute.

files.Title: Pulumi.ProviderResource
files.Summary: is a that implements CRUD operations for other custom resources. These resources are managed similarly to other resources, including the usual diffing and update semantics.

files.Title: Pulumi.RemoteArchive
files.Summary: A RemoteArchive is a file-based archive fetched from a remote location. The URI's scheme dictates the protocol for fetching the archive's contents: file:// is a local file (just like a FileArchive), http:// and https:// specify HTTP and HTTPS, respectively, and specific providers may recognize custom schemes.

files.Title: Pulumi.RemoteAsset
files.Summary: RemoteAsset is a kind of asset produced from a given URI string. The URI's scheme dictates the protocol for fetching contents: file:// specifies a local file, http:// and https:// specify HTTP and HTTPS, respectively. Note that specific providers may recognize alternative schemes; this is merely the base-most set that all providers support.

files.Title: Pulumi.Resource
files.Summary: Resource represents a class whose CRUD operations are implemented by a provider plugin.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceArgs
files.Summary: Base type for all resource argument classes.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceException
files.Summary: ResourceException can be used for terminating a program abruptly, specifically associating the problem with a Resource.Depending on the nature of the problem, clients can choose whether or not a call stack should be returned as well. This should be very rare, and would only indicate no usefulness of presenting that stack to the user.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceHook
files.Summary: A resource hook is a callback that can be registered to run at specific points in the resource lifecycle.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceHookArgs
files.Summary:
ResourceHookArgs represents the arguments passed to a resource hook. Depending on the hook type, only some of the new/old inputs/outputs are set. 

| Hook Type     | old_inputs | new_inputs | old_outputs | new_outputs |
| ------------- | ---------- | ---------- | ----------- | ----------- |
| before_create |            | ✓          |             |             |
| after_create  |            | ✓          |             | ✓           |
| before_update | ✓          | ✓          | ✓           |             |
| after_update  | ✓          | ✓          | ✓           | ✓           |
| before_delete | ✓          |            | ✓           |             |
| after_delete  | ✓          |            | ✓           |             |

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceHookBinding
files.Summary:
Binds s to a resource. The resource hooks will be invoked at specific points in the lifecycle of the resource.

Before* hooks that raise an exception will cause the resource operation to fail. After* hooks that raise an exception will log a warning, but do not cause the action or the deployment to fail.

When running pulumi destroy, BeforeDelete and AfterDelete resource hooks require the operation to run with --run-program, to ensure that the program which defines the hooks is available.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceHookCallback
files.Summary: ResourceHookCallback is a delegate that defines the signature of callback functions. Callbacks take a set of and an optional . Callbacks may raise an error by throwing an exception, which will be propagated to the resource operation that triggered the hook.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceHookOptions
files.Summary: Options for registering a .

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceOptions
files.Summary: ResourceOptions is a bag of optional settings that control a resource's behavior.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceTransform
files.Summary: ResourceTransform is the callback signature for . A transform is passed the same set of inputs provided to the constructor, and can optionally return back alternate values for the properties and/or options prior to the resource actually being created. The effect will be as though those properties and/or options were passed in place of the original call to the constructor. If the transform returns null, this indicates that the resource will not be transformed.

files.Title: Pulumi.ResourceTransformation
files.Summary: ResourceTransformation is the callback signature for . A transformation is passed the same set of inputs provided to the constructor, and can optionally return back alternate values for the properties and/or options prior to the resource actually being created. The effect will be as though those properties and/or options were passed in place of the original call to the constructor. If the transformation returns null, this indicates that the resource will not be transformed.

files.Title: Pulumi.RunException
files.Summary: RunException can be used for terminating a program abruptly, but resulting in a clean exit rather than the usual verbose unhandled error logic which emits the source program text and complete stack trace. This type should be rarely used and not be used by developers. The reason to make it public is for it to be assertable by testing frameworks. Ideally should always be used so that as many errors as possible can be associated with a Resource.

files.Title: Pulumi.Stack
files.Summary: Stack is the root resource for a Pulumi stack. Derive from this class to create your stack definitions.

files.Title: Pulumi.StackOptions
files.Summary: is a bag of optional settings that control a stack's behavior.

files.Title: Pulumi.StackReference
files.Summary: Manages a reference to a Pulumi stack and provides access to the referenced stack's outputs.

files.Title: Pulumi.StackReferenceArgs
files.Summary: The set of arguments for constructing a StackReference resource.

files.Title: Pulumi.StackReferenceOutputDetails
files.Summary: Holds a StackReference's output value. At most one of Value and SecretValue will be set.

files.Title: Pulumi.Stash
files.Summary: Stash stores an arbitrary value in the state.

files.Title: Pulumi.StashArgs
files.Summary: The set of arguments for constructing a resource.

files.Title: Pulumi.StringAsset
files.Summary: StringAsset is a kind of asset produced from an in-memory UTF8-encoded string.

files.Title: Pulumi.Testing.IMocks
files.Summary: Hooks to mock the engine that provide test doubles for offline unit testing of stacks.

files.Title: Pulumi.Testing.MockCallArgs
files.Summary: MockCallArgs for use in CallAsync

files.Title: Pulumi.Testing.MockRegisterResourceOutputsRequest
files.Summary: MockRegisterResourceOutputsRequest for use in RegisterOutputRequest

files.Title: Pulumi.Testing.MockResourceArgs
files.Summary: MockResourceArgs for use in NewResourceAsync

files.Title: Pulumi.Testing.TestOptions
files.Summary: Optional settings for .

files.Title: Pulumi.Union<T0, T1>
files.Summary:
Represents a Tagged Union.

This is used to hold a value that could take on several different, but fixed, types. Only one of the types can be in use at any one time. It can be thought of as a type that has several "cases," each of which should be handled correctly when that type is manipulated.

For example, a property that could either store a or a can be represented as Output<int, string>. The version of this is .

files.Title: Pulumi.Urn
files.Summary: An automatically generated logical URN, used to stably identify resources. These are created automatically by Pulumi to identify resources. They cannot be manually constructed.
